text: Imperially Designated Four Treasuries of Books, the twelfth category of the Zi section.

**Records of the Heavenly Era, Passed Down with Faith** **A Second Category of the Novel Genre: Strange Tales** **Abstract** Your subjects, the court officials, respectfully submit that *Records of the Heavenly Era, Passed Down with Faith* is a one-scroll work composed by Zheng Qi of the Tang Dynasty. Zheng Qi, with the courtesy name Yunwu, was from Xingyang. He passed the imperial examination and was repeatedly promoted to the position of Right Attendant of the Scattered Gentlemen. He was fond of composing poems and ballads, indirectly criticizing Emperor Zhaozong, and it was believed that he had deep knowledge, which led to his promotion to the position of Minister of Rites and member of the imperial council. This is the person referred to as "Zheng Wu, the After-the-Pause," whose actions as chancellor are known. According to the *Old History of the Tang Dynasty*, Zheng Qi once held the positions of Censor, Palace Attendant, Grain and Household Censor, Gold and Criminal Censor, and Right Justice Censor. However, the original manuscript of this book lists his position as Junior Censor of the Ministry of Personnel, which is not mentioned in his biography. Perhaps there is a gap or omission in the historical record. The book contains thirty-two stories of the Kaiyuan and Tianbao eras, all of which are recorded in the preface as being written during his spare time from official duties, seeking out lost or forgotten knowledge with the aim of absolute credibility, hence the title *Records of the Heavenly Era, Passed Down with Faith*. One of the stories recounts Emperor Xuanzong's leisurely activities in the southern part of the capital, where Wang Ju invited him to his home to plot against the family of Empress Wei. According to the *Old History of the Tang Dynasty*, Wang Ju was selected to serve as a chief clerk and visited the crown prince to offer advice, but there is no mention of him having previously visited Wang Ju's home. Sima Guang, in his *Comprehensive Mirror to Aid in Government*, also does not follow this account, but the *New History of the Tang Dynasty* incorporates it. However, during the time when the family of Empress Wei held power, Wang Ju was fleeing with the party of Wang Tongjiao to Jiangdu, so how could he have chosen to live in the area of Wei and Du? Zheng Qi's account seems unlikely to be factual, and it is perhaps for this reason that Sima Guang did not include it. Other stories, such as Zheng Qi's encounter with the god of Mount Hua, his dream of visiting the Moon Palace, and the supernatural abilities of Luō Gōngyuǎn and Yè Fǎshàn, also involve elements of the supernatural and are not entirely consistent with refined and elegant writing. However, because these stories have been widely circulated for a long time, many books discussing Tang Dynasty events often reference them, so they are included here as a contribution to the genre of strange tales. This translation was respectfully submitted in the twelfth month of the forty-sixth year of the Qianlong era.

The Chief Compilers [Minister] Ji Yun, [Minister] Lu Xixiong, [Minister] Sun Shiyi The Chief Proofreader [Minister] Lu Feicheng The Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries The Record of the Truths of Kaiyuan and Tianbao By Zheng Qi of the Tang Dynasty Who am I? Repeatedly holding the position of Lang, I have thought diligently about the classics to self-cultivate and encourage myself. I secretly believe that the stories of our dynasty were most prosperous during the Kaiyuan and Tianbao periods. I hold close the classics and laws, viewing state affairs through a narrow tube, participating in what I hear, but there may be omissions. The prosperity of the peaceful times cannot be allowed to fall. I take advantage of my spare time to search for and transmit these truths, calling it the *Record of the Truths of Kaiyuan and Tianbao*. A small and insignificant vessel, the fault of the Zhou tripod not being in harmony, how far is it? Those who are fond of learning should observe their intentions, forgive their foolishness, and recognize their genuine intentions. When the future emperor was living in his princely residence, he often played in the southern part of Chang'an, between Wei and Du, chasing hares and enjoying himself so much that he forgot to return, accompanied by several companions. When they were very tired, they rested under a large tree in the outskirts. Suddenly, a scholar invited the future emperor to his home. The scholar's home was poor, with only a wife and a donkey. After sitting for a short while, the scholar killed the donkey, chopped garlic, and prepared a feast of meat and wine, which was abundant. The future emperor looked at him with surprise and found him noble and extraordinary. He asked the scholar's name and learned that it was Wang Ju. From then on, whenever the future emperor visited Wei and Du, he would always stop by Wang Ju's house. Wang Ju's advice was in line with his thoughts, and they became closer and more intimate. When the Wei clan gained power, the future emperor was very worried and secretly spoke to Wang Ju. Wang Ju said, "If there is chaos, then kill them. What else is there to doubt?" The future emperor then accepted Wang Ju's plan, quelled the disaster, and was repeatedly promoted to the position of Zhongshu Shilang, indeed included in the temple sacrifices.

The emperor showed extraordinary affection for his brothers, often thinking of making a long pillow to sleep with them. Whenever one of his brothers was ill, he would go without food all day and stay awake all night, and his worries were visible on his face. His advisors tried to persuade him to eat, but he would say, "My brothers are like my hands and feet. If I don't take care of them, I am neglecting myself. How can I think of enjoying good food or sleeping peacefully?" In the eastern capital, he built a residence for five of his brothers, and in the western capital, he constructed the Flower and Bud Pavilion, a place for gatherings and feasts. He met with his brothers almost every day, discussing Confucian teachings and principles, and engaging in ball games, hunting, and gambling. They recited poetry, enjoyed meals, laughed, and joked, never showing any signs of weariness. The brotherly love of ancient emperors could not compare to his. In the early years of the Kaiyuan era, the emperor was diligent in governing and reforming, eliminating errors and corruption. Within six or seven years, the empire was well-governed, with clear rivers and calm seas, abundant goods, and prosperous customs. The Western Regions were all pacified and became prefectures and counties. From the Kaiyuan Gate, the land stretched for more than ten thousand miles to the west, with taxes collected from the rivers and lakes. The imperial treasuries were filled with treasures, and the four directions were bountiful. The people were prosperous, with over ten million households, and rice cost only three or four coins per dou. Young men did not know what weapons were, and there was no need to carry food while traveling. Wonders and auspicious signs occurred repeatedly, and messengers arrived frequently. The people were joyful, and the emperor was still vigilant and cautious, declining the throne several times. At that time, Liu Yan, who was eight years old, submitted a book on the eastern seal. The emperor found it remarkable and ordered the chancellor to set questions and test him in the Secretariat. Zhang Yue and Yuan Dan all highly recommended him. The emperor, impressed by Liu Yan's exceptional talent, invited him to the inner palace, where the six palaces watched in admiration. The empress, Yang Guifei, sat Liu Yan on her knee, personally drawing his eyebrows and tying his hair in a topknot. People in the palace threw fruits and flowers, too many to count. Soon after, Liu Yan was appointed as a proofreader in the Secretariat of the Imperial Academy.

In the early years of Kaiyuan, there was a great locust plague in Shandong. Yao Yuanzong requested to send officials to capture and bury the locusts. The emperor said, "Locusts are a heavenly calamity, truly caused by our lack of virtue. You request to capture locusts, but does this not go against our principles and harm righteousness?" Yao Yuanzong replied, "I have heard that the method of capturing locusts is to cast them into the flames, as stated in the *Dàyán* poem. Ancient people practiced this method before us, and Your Majesty can use it now. The ancients practiced it to ensure agricultural stability, and Your Majesty uses it to eliminate pests. I have heard that ensuring agricultural stability does not harm righteousness. If agriculture is stable, then crops will be abundant; if pests are eliminated, then people will enjoy prosperity. Ensuring agricultural stability and eliminating pests are major affairs of the state. I hope Your Majesty will carefully consider this." The emperor said, "If we follow ancient practices, we can save the times, which is what I intend." Thus, the plan was implemented. At the time, officials and commoners alike believed it was not possible, but the emperor said, "I have discussed this with my wise minister and decided on this course of action. Anyone who dares to argue will be put to death." That year, the officials reported that they had captured and buried a total of [missing number] hundred [missing number] ten thousand stones of locusts. There was no famine, and the people of the country benefited greatly.

The emperor ascended Mount Tai to perform the ritual of Fengshan. A white mule from Yizhou arrived, clean, bright, and of extraordinary beauty. The emperor then personally rode it. The mule was gentle, well-trained, and comfortable, showing no signs of fatigue from ascending and descending. After the ceremony was completed, the emperor rode it back down. As soon as it reached the bottom of the hill, it rested for a short while. Officials reported that the white mule died without any apparent illness. The emperor was amazed and saddened by this. It was posthumously given the title of General White Mule. The emperor ordered officials to prepare a coffin and stone burial mound, located about one mile north of the Fengshan altar, and it still exists today. The imperial entourage arrived at Huayin. The emperor saw the mountain god several miles away, welcoming him. The emperor asked those around him, but none saw anything. He then summoned various shamans to ask where the god was. Only the old shaman Amapo reported that the Third Prince was on the left side of the road, wearing red hair and purple clothes, waiting for the emperor. The emperor smiled at this. He then ordered Amapo to tell the god to return first. When the emperor arrived at the temple, he saw the god, with his bow and arrows, kneeling under a large pine tree in the southeast corner of the courtyard. He then summoned Amapo again to ask, and she responded as she had seen. The emperor added more respect and instructed Amapo to convey his intentions and then returned. He issued an edict to first visit the mountain and declare it the King of Gold Heaven. He also wrote the inscription for the stele himself to honor and distinguish it. The stele was over fifty feet tall, more than ten feet wide, and four to five feet thick. No other stele in the world could compare to it. The rest of the stele carved the names of the imperial entourage, princes, nobles, and officials, and the design was magnificent and intricate, unmatched in the world. When he was a prince, he had a striking appearance and extraordinary spirit. He once scolded Wu Youji in the court, saying, "This court is my family's court. How dare you act like a scorpion and look around suspiciously?" Empress Wu heard this.

The emperor was surprised and looked at the child repeatedly, saying, "This child's demeanor will surely make him our family's peaceful emperor." The people of Western Liang state are fond of music and created a new tune called "Liangzhou." During the Kaiyuan era, it was presented to the emperor, who summoned the princes to a side hall to watch together. At the end of the song, the princes congratulated and danced, praising it, but only Prince Ning did not bow. The emperor asked him, and Prince Ning replied, "This song is indeed beautiful, but I have heard something about it. Music starts with the palace tone, spreads to the commercial tone, and completes in the angular, Zheng, and Yu tones. This song has a weak palace tone and too much commercial tone, which is chaotic and violent. I have heard that the palace tone represents the ruler and the commercial tone represents the minister. If the palace tone is weak, the ruler's power is low; if the commercial tone is excessive, the minister's actions are僭越, which is disrespectful to the ruler. It starts from subtle signs, manifests in sounds, and appears in songs and people's lives. I fear that one day it will lead to rebellion and disorder." The emperor listened silently. When the An Lushan and Shi Siming rebellion occurred, the whole country was in chaos, showing Prince Ning's keen understanding of music. During the Tianbao era, the emperor faced difficulties in transporting grain due to dangerous river routes, so he ordered the construction of a stone channel along the north mountain to avoid the rapid currents. It was named the Tianbao River, saving fifty thousand laborers each year and eliminating the risk of drowning or delays. It was praised throughout the land. The river is about five miles long, four to five zhang wide, and three to four zhang deep, all carved from hard stone. The craftsmen found an ancient iron plate in the stone, three chi long, with the words "Pinglu" in ancient script. The emperor was surprised and kept it in the inner treasury, then ordered the renaming of Hebei county to Pinglu county to commemorate the event.

The emperor presided over a grand feast at the Qinzhi Tower, allowing the common people to watch various performances. The crowd was so dense that people were jostling each other, and the imperial guards with white rods rained down blows but could not stop the chaos. The emperor was concerned about this and asked the strongman, "I think the country is prosperous and there are no major events, so I hold grand feasts to enjoy with the people. I did not realize that the common people were so noisy and disorderly. What can you do to stop this?" The strongman replied, "I cannot do anything, Your Majesty." The emperor then summoned Yan An to manage the performance area. Yan An circled the wide performance area and drew on the ground with a tablet, saying to the crowd, "Anyone who violates this will be executed." As a result, throughout the five-day feast, everyone pointed to the marked area and said, "This is Yan Gong's territory, and no one dares to trespass." Initially, Su Gui did not know Ting, who worked alongside hired servants in the stables. One day, a guest visited Su Gui and stopped at the entrance. Ting, holding a broom, hurried to the courtyard and dropped a document. The guest picked it up and saw that it was a poem about the Kunlun slave. The poem read, "Ten black fingers, two ears like peaches, two spoons." The guest was amazed by Ting's talent and stayed to talk with Su Gui. After some conversation, the guest recited the poem and described Ting's appearance, asking, "Who is this person? Is he not one of your illegitimate offspring? If you treat him with respect and promote him, he will surely become a distinguished son of the Su family." Su Gui began to treat Ting more favorably. Just then, someone presented Su Gui with a rabbit hanging in the corridor. Su Gui then summoned Ting to compose a poem about it. Ting immediately presented a poem: "The rabbit is dead, its fur is gone, held up by a bamboo pole. Try to look at it in a bright mirror; it is no different from the moon." Su Gui was greatly surprised and impressed, suddenly increasing his respect and courtesy for Ting. From then on, Ting's knowledge became increasingly advanced, and his writings surpassed his contemporaries. When the emperor quelled internal troubles, edicts and decrees were issued continuously, and none of them were written by anyone else except Ting, who was known as a minor official.

Upon ascending Mount Tai, the emperor turned his chariot and proceeded along the road to Shanxi Province. The elderly people from far and near, carrying loads and offering drinks, came to greet him. The emperor personally greeted them, asked about their well-being, accepted their gifts, and rewarded them according to their status. Some of the elderly people were previously acquainted with the emperor, and he gave them wine and food, talking about old times with them. Whenever passing through villages, the emperor ordered inquiries into the homes of widows, the elderly, and those who were sick or bereaved, and extended condolences and assistance. The elderly people were delighted and all looked up to him, begging him to stay. As the golden bridge and imperial road wound and turned, the banners and flags were bright and clean, and the guard was well-organized. The emperor turned and said to those around him, "Zhang Yue said that there were thirty thousand troops, banners stretching for thousands of miles, from the west of Shanxi to Taiyuan, and he saw the Later Earth Stele, which is truly the work of a talented person." Those around him all shouted, "Long live the emperor!" The emperor then ordered Wu Daoxuan, Wei Wutian, and Chen Hong to jointly create a painting of the golden bridge, the emperor's face, and his horse that shines at night. Chen Hong was in charge of the bridge, mountains, rivers, vehicles, people, trees, birds, utensils, and curtains. Wu Daoxuan was in charge of dogs, horses, donkeys, mules, cattle, sheep, camels, monkeys, pigs, and other four-legged animals. When the painting was completed, it was considered a "triple marvel."

The emperor traveled to Shu, and the imperial entourage arrived at the Sword Gate, where the rocks on both sides of the gate were steep and precipitous. The emperor said to his attendants, "The Sword Gate is so treacherous that from ancient times to the present, successive defeats and losses have occurred. Is it not because of virtue, not because of danger, that such things have happened?" He then paused to compose a poem, saying, "The Sword Pass stretches high into the sky, and the imperial carriage returns from the hunt. The green screens of a thousand feet converged, the red barriers were opened by the Five Dukes, the flags turned as they passed through the dense vegetation, and the divine clouds brushed past the horses. Taking advantage of the right time lies in virtue, alas, you have the talent to inscribe." The poem was inscribed on a stone wall in 758 CE by the local official贾深 and still exists today. 贺知章, a prominent poet and statesman, had a high reputation. When he retired to his hometown in Wu, the emperor held him in great esteem and treated him with special favor. Before贺知章 left, he wept and said to the emperor, "What do you wish for me?" The emperor replied, "The essence of the way is trust. When one walks the path of trust, one thinks of righteousness. Your son will surely be a person of trust and righteousness. I will name him 'Fu.'"贺知章 bowed twice and accepted the name. Later, he said to others, "Why is the emperor joking with me? The people of Wu say that 'Fu' means 'under the melon' for a son. Is this not calling me a melon son?"

The emperor, while sitting on the throne, pressed his stomach with his fingers. After the court session, Gao Lishi approached and said, "Your Majesty, you have often pressed your stomach with your fingers. Is it not because your health is slightly unwell?" The emperor replied, "No, it is not. Last night, I dreamed of traveling to the Moon Palace, where the immortals entertained me with the music of the highest purity, which was clear and resonant, almost beyond what can be heard in the mortal world. After drinking deeply, they played various pieces of music to send me back. The melody was melancholy and moving, lingering in my ears. I returned and sought it out with a jade flute, and managed to capture it completely. While sitting on the throne, I fear I might forget it, so I keep the jade flute in my bosom and occasionally gesture with my fingers to recall it. It is not that I am unwell." Gao Lishi bowed twice and congratulated the emperor, saying, "This is an extraordinary matter! May I request that Your Majesty play it for me once?" The sound was ethereal and indescribable. Gao Lishi bowed again and asked for the name of the tune. The emperor smiled and said, "This tune is called 'Purple Cloud Returns.' It has since been recorded in the musical canon and is now inscribed on stone by the Imperial Music Bureau." After ascending Mount Tai, the emperor proceeded to荥yang and saw a black dragon on the Zhanran River. He ordered the bow and arrow to shoot at it, and the dragon submerged. Since then, the Zhanran River has been hidden underground for over a century. According to historical records, the Zhanran River is the same as the Ji River, which overflowed and formed the city of荥yang, hence the name Zhanran. The *Zuo Zhuan* (Commentary of Zuo) mentions that the Chu army crossed the Zhanran River. On Mount Hua, above the Yun Tai Temple, there is a mountain that rises in a shape resembling half a jar, named Wengdu Peak. The emperor admired its height and remoteness and wanted to carve the characters "開元" (meaning "the beginning of the Kaiyuan era") into the mountain with white stone, so that they could be seen from over a hundred li. However, the censor officials advised against it, and so it was stopped.

The Emperor discovered a divine symbol inscribed on a white stone at the ancient Hangu Pass in Hongnong, which read "cheng cheng." Those who understood the symbol interpreted it to mean a period of forty-eight years. At the time of obtaining the treasure, people said, "The treasure was obtained in Hongnong. Was the treasure obtained there?" Today, it is sung that the year of obtaining the treasure was changed to the era name Tianbao. The Emperor favored An Lushan as his son and often enjoyed his company with the empress in the palace. Every time An Lushan sat down, he did not bow to the Emperor but bowed to the empress. The Emperor asked, "Why does this man not bow to me but to the empress? What is his intention?" An Lushan replied, "In the triracial barbarian's culture, they know of a mother but not a father." The Emperor laughed and let it go. An Lushan was a large and fat man with a big belly. The Emperor once asked, "What is inside this man's belly that makes it so large?" An Lushan answered, "There is nothing else in my belly except a loyal heart." The Emperor, moved by his sincerity, grew even closer to him. When Yi Xing was about to die, he left a sealed package and ordered his disciples to present it to the Emperor. The Emperor opened it and saw that it contained Sichuan Angelica root. Initially, the Emperor did not understand its significance, but when he visited Sichuan and returned, he realized the subtle meaning and deeply admired it. Lu Gongyuan had many secret arts and was most skilled in the art of transforming into different forms. The Emperor studied with Lu Gongyuan and even though he taught and received, he would not fully reveal its essence. Every time the Emperor practiced with him, no one could see the transformation. However, if the Emperor tried on his own or if his clothes, belt, or headband were visible, the palace women always knew where the Emperor was. The Emperor was angry and ordered a strongman to wrap him in oil-soaked cloth, place him under a press, crush him to death, and bury him. Not many days later, a central messenger returned from Sichuan and met Lu Gongyuan on the road, riding a donkey and laughing, telling the messenger, "The Emperor's play was so cruel."

Wan Hui is a person from Wenxiang. His spiritual use is insufficient, so he is called foolish and stupid. He knows nothing, and even his parents treat him like a pig or a dog. His brother was conscripted to serve in Anxi, and there was no news. His parents thought he was truly dead and cried day and night, worrying about him. Wan Hui suddenly looked at his father, deeply moved, and knelt to say, "Are you not crying because you are worried about your brother?" His parents said, "Indeed." Wan Hui said, "Carefully think about what my brother needs—clothing, food, and other necessities—and prepare them all. I will go to see him." Suddenly, one day he left in the morning with the prepared items and returned home in the evening. He told his parents, "My brother is safe and well." They opened the book and saw that it was a letter from his brother. The whole family was amazed. From Hongnong to Anxi is more than ten thousand miles. Because he traveled ten thousand miles and returned, he is called Wan Hui. He usually looks foolish and stupid, but suddenly he has extraordinary foresight and unusual insights that amaze people. When the emperor was in his princely residence or traveling among the people, Wan Hui would shout, "The emperor is coming!" or "A sage is coming!" In the places he predicted, the emperor would indeed pass by after a few days. Anle Princess is the youngest sister of the emperor. She was close to the powerful Wei family, who were as hot as fire. People on the road were afraid of her. Wan Hui saw her carriage and horse and spat on the ground, saying, "She is filled with blood and cannot be approached. Her doom will come soon." The emperor knew that Wan Hui was not an ordinary person and ordered two palace attendants to serve him day and night. He also specially commanded that a portrait of Wan Hui be drawn in the Academy of Literature.

The Taoist priest Ye Fashan excelled in the art of talismanic magic. The emperor repeatedly honored him with the title of Minister of Ceremonies and treated him with great respect. Ye Fashan resided at Xuanzhen Temple, where he once had dozens of court officials visit him. They relaxed and stayed, thinking about wine, when suddenly someone knocked on the door and announced, "Mr. Qu is here." Ye Fashan sent someone to say, "I have court officials here and cannot spare the time to greet you. Please visit another time." Before the words were finished, a handsome young man entered with a haughty look, about twenty years old, with a plump and fair appearance. He laughed and bowed to the assembly, taking a seat at the end of the table. He spoke loudly and confidently, citing ancient examples, and the entire audience was astonished and fearful. After a while, he suddenly stood up and turned around. Ye Fashan said to the assembly, "This young man has entered so abruptly and speaks so eloquently. Is he not possessed by a demon or ghost? Let's try to avoid him." Mr. Qu returned, clasping his wrists and striking his palms, and the debate became sharp and intense, his momentum unstoppable. Ye Fashan secretly struck him with a small sword, and he fell from his hand and dropped to the ground, transforming into a jar of wine. The entire assembly was shocked and fearful, and they quickly looked at the place where he had fallen. It was a jar filled with strong wine. They all laughed and drank it, finding the taste very excellent. The guests drank the wine and bowed to the bottle, saying, "The taste of Qu is unforgettable." The emperor appointed Pei Kuan as the governor of Henan. Pei Kuan had a fondness for Buddhism and studied under Master Puji, visiting him every day and night. One day, Pei Kuan visited Master Puji, who said, "I have some matters to attend to and cannot spare the time for a long conversation. Please wait a while and rest." Pei Kuan then dismissed his attendants and stayed in an empty room. He saw Master Puji clean the main hall, burn incense, and sit upright. Before long, he heard continuous knocking and said that Master Yin had arrived. Master Yin entered, bowed, and touched Master Puji's feet. After bowing, he whispered in his ear. His appearance was extremely respectful, and Master Puji merely looked at him and said, "Nothing is impossible." After the conversation, he bowed again and repeated the same words three times. Master Puji only said, "Yes, yes." After Master Yin finished speaking, he descended the stairs, entered the southern hall, and closed the door. Master Puji then slowly instructed his disciples to sound the bell, and Master Yin was the one who had come.

A monk had attained Nirvana, and those around him rushed to look at him, just as he had said. Later, he passed away peacefully, and Kuan, wearing mourning clothes, buried him. On the day of the funeral, Kuan walked out of the city to see him off, and this was heavily criticized by the gentry. Kuan's son, Xu, was again appointed as the governor of Henan, and he always enjoyed humorous conversation, often writing unusual or eccentric things. Once, someone submitted a petition with a mistake, and Xu judged it by saying, "Those on the other side seem to be on that side," which was confusing and ambiguous. Another time, a woman submitted a petition about a cat, stating, "If it is a cat, then it is a son; if it is not a cat, then it is not a son." Xu laughed and judged the case, saying, "The cat doesn't recognize its owner. Beside me, grabbing the old guard. The two families don't need to fight. Let me take the cat." He accepted the cat, and the disputants also laughed. An Lushan initially served as a messenger for Zhang Han, a high-ranking official. Han ordered An Lushan to wash his feet, and Han had a black mole on his foot. An Lushan secretly looked at it while washing his feet, and Han smiled and said, "The mole is a sign of my nobility. Only you looked at it, could you also have one?" An Lushan replied, "I am a lowly person. Unfortunately, both my feet have moles larger than yours," and Han was amazed and treated him more favorably. They agreed to become adoptive father and son, and Han recommended and favored An Lushan. Venerable Bodhiruci arrived from India, and he was introduced to the emperor, who respected and trusted him. The emperor asked, "Master, having traveled far, is tired and wants to rest somewhere." Venerable Bodhiruci replied, "When I was in India, I heard that Ximing Temple's Master Xuan has the highest standards of precepts, and I wish to stay with him." The emperor agreed. Master Xuan strictly adhered to precepts, practiced asceticism, and maintained purity, while Venerable Bodhiruci drank alcohol, ate meat, and spoke casually.

Xuanlü was often drunk and noisy, defiling the bedding, which made him quite dissatisfied. One night, he was picking lice from his body and was about to throw them away when the Three Treasures, half-drunk, called out repeatedly, "Kill the Buddha's son!" Xuanlü realized that the young man was a divine being and immediately straightened his clothes, performed a bow, and treated him as a teacher. Xuanlü was extremely diligent and hardworking. He often fell while walking at night, and one such time, he suddenly felt someone supporting his feet. Looking around, he saw a young man. Xuanlü quickly asked, "Who are you, and why are you here in the middle of the night?" The young man replied, "I am not an ordinary person; I am the son of King Vaisravana, Prince Nata. Due to the Dharma, I have been protecting the monk for a long time." Xuanlü said, "I have no business that would trouble the prince's divine power. If there is anything from the Western Regions that can be used for Buddhist activities, I would like the prince to bring it." The prince responded, "I have a Buddha's tooth relic, which I would willingly offer even if it meant sacrificing my head and eyes." Xuanlü requested the relic, which is now housed in the Chongsheng Temple. The Empress Consort Taizhen was particularly skilled in playing the stone chime and the drum, with a clear and new sound that surpassed even the best musicians of the Imperial Music Bureau. The emperor ordered the collection of green jade from Lantian to be carved into instruments, which were then adorned with gold, pearls, and other precious materials. He also had two gold lions cast in a fierce and dynamic posture, each weighing over 200 pounds, to support the instruments. These were beautifully decorated and crafted with divine skill, unmatched at the time. After the emperor's visit to Shu and his return to the capital, many musical instruments were lost, but the jade chime was still there. Moved by its presence, the emperor ordered it to be sent to the Imperial Music Bureau, where it is still kept today.

The emperor's favored concubine suddenly had a dream where someone invited her to drink freely. Secretly guarded and allowed her to drink until she was full, then returned home, she would always sweat and feel tired. Later, she calmly and completely told the emperor about it. The emperor said, "This must be the work of a magician or sorcerer. If you go again, just mark it with something convenient." That night, she slept soundly and suddenly went again. Half-drunk, she saw a stone inkstone in front of her and secretly stamped her handprint on the curved room's screen. She woke up and reported everything to the emperor. The emperor then secretly sent someone to search all the palaces and temples. One day, at the eastern gate temple, they found the screen with her handprint still on it, but the Taoist had already fled. The military officer Liu Wenshu from the Anxi region was eloquent and good at responding to imperial questions. The emperor always praised him. Liu Wenshu had a beard under his chin and looked like an ape. The emperor ordered Huang Fanchuo to mock him. Liu Wenshu strongly disliked being called an ape. He secretly bribed Huang Fanchuo to ask him not to say it. Huang Fanchuo asked and then mocked him saying, "Pitiful, good Liu Wenshu, his beard and chin are separate. Let Liu Wenshu's face not be like a monkey. Monkeys are stronger than Liu Wenshu." The emperor knew about the bribe and laughed at it. The abandoned foreigner's house on the south street of Pingkang Ward was once the old residence of Li Linfu. Li Linfu built a separate hall behind the main hall with a curved shape resembling a crescent moon, named it the Moon Hall. The wood and earth were beautiful and exquisite, unmatched at the time. Whenever Li Linfu wanted to destroy a family, he would enter the Moon Hall, deeply ponder, and happily exit, but the family would surely not survive. When he was about to be defeated, Li Linfu saw something like a person move in the hall, covered in hair like a pig, with sharp teeth and claws over three feet long, striking his eyes like lightning and glaring angrily. Li Linfu shouted repeatedly, but it didn't move, so he ordered arrows, and the hairy person laughed and jumped into the front hall. The maid in the hall met it and died suddenly, and the good horses in the stable all died within a few days, and Li Linfu also died.

The Consort Yang often offended the emperor with her jealous and flattering words, which greatly angered him. He then summoned Gao Lishi and ordered him to send her home in a luxurious carriage. The consort regretted her actions and wept bitterly, drawing a knife to cut her hair and presenting it to Gao Lishi, saying, "Jewels, precious stones, and rare items, all bestowed by the emperor, are insufficient for offering. Only my hair, born of my parents, can express my feelings. I hope to hold this to express my slightest longing and attachment." The emperor received the hair and shed tears, feeling compassion, and immediately ordered Gao Lishi to bring her back. In the early years of Tianbao, the emperor toured the Huaqing Palace. There was Liu Zhaoxia who presented a congratulatory poem for the emperor's visit to the hot springs. The style of the poem was bold and varied, mixed with humor, and much of it is not recorded here. Here is a brief excerpt: "If it is the second year of Tianbao, after the tenth month and before the twelfth, the imperial officials prepare the necessary supplies, and the emperor's carriage arrives at the hot springs. The gates of heaven open, revealing the chariots of the immortals. The imperial carriage emerges, followed by an array of armored soldiers in a grand procession. One team in blue, another in yellow, bears tread on chests, and leopards grab their backs. A red team, a team of embroidered robes, jade-adorned horses, and golden saddles. The clouds of virtue stretch straight, grasping the marrow of Pangu and the flesh of Nüwa. How could the thousand emperors of ancient times compare to me, the emperor of today? In his own account, he says, 'There are strange and unlucky setbacks on the road, leading to wild and reckless behavior. Though my bones are short, my skills and arts are long. In dreams, I return to wealth and honor, but upon waking, I remain in sorrow. Today is a once-in-a-thousand-years encounter; bow your head, do not be five or six angles.' The emperor found this poem strange and intended to reward Liu Zhaoxia. He ordered Liu Zhaoxia to remove the lines 'five or six angles' and submitted a response: 'I wrote this poem with divine assistance, believing that my writing was flawless and my pen did not pause. I do not wish to change it under heaven.' The emperor replied, 'You are indeed a poor and thin person,' and granted him the position of Spring Official and Guard Captain on the left."
钦定四库全书 子部十二

开天传信记 小説家二【异闻之属】提要 【臣】等谨案开天传信记一卷唐郑綮撰綮字蕴武荥阳人登进士第累官右散骑常侍好以诗謡托讽昭宗意其有所蕴蓄擢为礼部侍郎同中书门下平章事所谓歇后郑五作宰相时事可知者即其人也旧唐书本传称綮尝厯监察殿中仓戸二员外金刑右司三郎而是书原本首署其官为吏部员外郎本传顾未之及或史文有所脱漏欤书中皆记开元天寳故事凡三十二条目序称簿领之暇捜求遗逸期于必信故以传信为名其纪明皇戏 游城南王琚延过其家谋诛韦氏一条据唐书琚传乃琚选补主簿过谢太子乗机进説以除太平公主并无先过琚家之事司马光作通鉴亦不从是书惟新唐书兼采之然韦氏称制时琚方以王同皎党亡命江都安得复卜居韦杜綮所纪恐非事实宜为通鉴所不取又如华隂见岳神梦游月宫罗公逺形叶法善符箓诸事亦语涉神怪未能尽出于雅驯然行世既久诸书言唐事者多沿用之故録以备小説之一种焉乾隆四十六年十二月恭校上

总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅 总 校 官【臣】陆 费 墀 钦定四库全书 开天传信记 唐 郑綮 撰 余何为者也累忝台郎思勤坟典用自修励窃以国朝故事莫盛于开元天寳之际服膺简防管窥王业参于聴闻或有阙焉承平之盛不可殒坠辄因簿领之暇捜求遗逸传于必信名曰开天传信记斗筲微器周鼎不节之咎何已遐乎好事者观其志寛其愚是其心也上于藩邸时毎戱逰城南韦杜之间因逐狡兎意乐忘返与其徒十数一 饮倦甚休息于封部大下适有书生延上过其家家贫止于村妻一驴而已上坐未久书生杀驴防蒜备馔酒肉霶霈上顾而竒之及与语磊落不凡问其姓名乃王琚也自是上毎逰韦杜间必过琚家琚所谘议合意益亲善焉及韦氏専制上忧甚独密言于琚琚曰乱则杀之又何疑也上遂纳琚之谋戡定祸难累拜为中书侍郎实预配享焉

上于诸王友爱特甚常思作长枕与诸王同起卧诸王有疾上辄终日不食终夜不寝忧形于色左右或开谕进食上曰弟兄吾手足也手足不理吾身废矣何暇更思美食安寝耶上于东都起五王宅于上都制花萼相辉之楼盖为诸王为防集宴乐之地上与诸王靡日不防聚防讲经义论理道间以毬猎蒱博赋诗饮食欢笑戱谑未尝怠惰近古帝王友爱之道无与比也 开元初上励精理道铲革讹弊不六七年天下大治河清海晏物殷俗阜安西诸国悉平为郡县自开逺门西行亘地万余里入河隍之赋税左右蔵库财物山积不可胜较四方丰稔百姓殷富管户一千余万米一斗三四文丁壮之人不识兵器路不拾遗行者不囊粮竒瑞叠应重驿麕至人情欣欣然感登岱告成之事上犹惕励不已为譲者数四焉是时刘晏年八嵗献东封书上覧而竒之命宰相出题就中书试验张说源干等咸宠荐上以晏间生秀妙引晏于内殿纵六宫观防贵妃坐晏于膝上亲为画眉总丱髻宫中人投果遗花者不可胜数也寻拜晏秘书省正字

开元初山东大蝗姚元崇请分遣使捕蝗埋之上曰蝗天灾也诚由不徳而致焉卿请捕蝗得无违而伤义乎元崇进曰臣闻大田诗曰秉畀炎火者捕蝗之术也古人行之于前陛下用之于后古人行之所以安农陛下用之所以除害臣闻安农非伤义也农安则物丰除害则人丰乐兴农去害有国家之大事也幸陛下熟思之上喜曰事既师古用可救时是朕心也遂行之时中外咸以为不可上谓左右曰吾与贤相讨论已定捕蝗之事敢议者死是嵗所司结奏捕蝗虫凡【缺】百【缺】余万石时无饥馑天下赖焉

上将登封泰山益州进白骡至洁朗丰润权竒伟异上遂亲乗之柔习安便不知登降之倦告成礼毕复乗而下才下山坳休息未乆而有司言白骡无疾而殪上叹异之諡曰白骡将军命有司具槥椟叠石为墓在封禅坛北一里余于今在焉 车驾次华隂上见岳神数里迎谒上问左右莫之见遂诏诸巫问神安在独老巫阿马婆奏云三郎在路左朱发紫衣迎陛下上顾笑之仍勑阿马婆勑神先归上至庙见神櫜鞬俯伏庭东南大栢树下又召阿马婆问之对如上见上加敬礼命阿马婆致意而旋降诏先诣岳封为金天王仍上自书制碑文以宠异之其碑髙五十余尺濶丈余厚四五尺天下碑莫比也其余刻扈从太子王公以下百官名氏制作壮丽巧无比伦焉上为皇孙时风表瓌异神采英迈尝于朝堂叱武攸暨曰朝堂我家朝堂汝得恣蜂虿而狼顾耶则天闻

而惊异之再三顾曰此儿气概终当为吾家太平天子也西凉州习好音乐制新曲曰凉州开元中列上献上召诸王便殿同观曲终诸王贺舞蹈称善独宁王不拜上顾问之宁王进曰此曲虽嘉臣有闻焉夫音者始于宫散于商成于角征羽莫不根柢囊槖于宫商也斯曲也宫离而少征商乱而加暴臣闻宫君也商臣也宫不胜则君势卑商有余则臣事僣卑则逼下僣则犯上发于忽微形于音声播于歌咏见之于人事臣恐一日有播越之祸悖逼之患莫不兆于斯曲也上闻之黙然及安史作乱华夏鼎沸所以见宁王审音之妙也 天寳中上以三河道险束漕运艰难乃傍北山凿石为月河以避湍急名曰天寳河嵗省运夫五十万人无覆溺淹滞之患天下称之其河东西径直长五里余濶四五丈深三四丈皆凿坚石匠人于石得古铁鍱长三尺余上有平陆二字皆文也上异之蔵于内库遂命改河北县为平陆县旌其事也

上御勤政楼大酺纵士庶观防百戯竞作人物塡咽金吾卫士白棒雨下不能制止上患之谓力士曰吾以海内丰稔四方无事故盛为宴乐与百姓同欢不知下人喧乱如此汝何以止之力士曰臣不能也陛下召严安之处分打塲以臣所见必有可观上从之安之到则周行广塲以手板画地示众曰犯此者死以是终五日酺宴咸指其地画曰严公界境无一人敢犯者 蘓瓌初未知颋常处颋于马廐中与佣仆杂作一日有客诣瓌候于防所颋拥篲趋庭遗坠文书客取视之乃咏昆仑奴诗也其词曰指头十挺墨耳朶两张匙客心异之而瓌出与客淹留客笑语之余因咏其诗并言形貌问何人非足下宗族庶孽耶若加礼収举必苏氏之令子也瓌自是稍稍亲之适有人献瓌兎悬于廊庑间瓌乃召颋咏之立呈诗曰兎子死防殚持来挂竹竿试将明镜照何异月中防瓌大惊竒骤加礼敬颋由是学问日新文章盖代及上平内难一夕间制诏络绎无非颋出代称小许公也

上封泰山回车驾次上党路之父老担壷浆逺近迎谒上皆亲加存问受其献馈锡赉有差父老有先与上相识者上悉赐酒食与之话旧故过村部必令询访孤老丧疾之家加吊恤之父老忻忻然莫不瞻戴叩乞驻留焉及车金桥御路萦转上见数十里间旌纛鲜洁羽卫整齐顾谓左右曰张説言勒兵三十万旌旗千里间陜右上党至于太原见后土碑真才子也左右皆称万嵗上遂诏呉道韦无忝陈闳令同制金桥图圣容及上所乗照夜白马陈闳主之桥梁山水车舆人物草树鴈鸟器仗帷幕呉道主之狗马骡驴牛羊骆驼猫猴猪四足之韦无忝主之图成时为三絶焉

上幸蜀车驾次劔门门左右岩壁峭絶上谓侍臣曰劒门天险若此自古及今败亡相继岂非在徳不在险耶因驻跸题诗曰劒阁横空峻銮舆出狩回翠屏千仞合丹障五丁开灌木萦旗转僊云拂马来乗时方在徳嗟尔勒铭才其诗至徳二年普安郡太守贾深勒于石壁今存焉 贺知章秘书监有髙名吿老归呉中上嘉重之每别优异焉知章将行涕泣辞上曰何所欲知章曰臣有男未有定名幸陛下赐之归为乡里荣上曰为道之要莫信孚者信也履信思乎顺卿子必信顺之人也宜名之曰孚知章再拜而受命知章乆而谓人曰上何谑我耶呉人孚乃下为子岂非呼我为子耶

上尝坐朝以手指上下按其腹退朝髙力士进曰陛下向来数以手指按其腹岂非圣体小不安耶上曰非也吾昨夜梦游月宫诸僊娯予以上清之乐寥亮清越殆非人间所闻也酣醉乆之合奏诸乐以送吾归其曲凄楚动人杳杳在耳吾回以玉笛寻之尽得之矣坐朝之际虑忽遗忘故懐玉笛时以手指上下寻非不安力士再拜贺曰非常之事也愿陛下为臣一奏之其声寥寥然不可名言也力士又再拜且请其名上笑言此曲名紫云回遂载于乐章今太常刻石在焉 上封泰山进次荥阳旃然河上见黒龙命弧矢射之矢发龙潜防自尔旃然伏流于今百余年矣按旃然即济水也溢而为荥遂名旃然左传云楚师济于旃然是也华岳云台观中方之上有山崛起半瓮之状名曰瓮肚峯上赏望嘉其髙迥欲于峯腹大凿开元二字塡以白石令百余里望见諌官上言乃止

上于农古函谷闗得寳符白石文正成乗字识者解之云乗者四十八年得寳之时天下言之曰得寳农得寳耶于今唱之得寳之年遂改天寳也 上幸爱禄山为子尝与贵妃于便殿同乐禄山每就坐不拜上而拜妃上顾问此胡不拜我而拜妃子意何在也禄山奏曰胡家即知有母不知有父故也上笑而舍之禄山丰肥大腹上尝问曰此胡腹中更有何物其大如是禄山寻声应曰腹中更无他物惟赤心尔上以言诚而益亲善之 一行将卒留物一封命弟子进于上发而视之乃蜀当归也上初不谕及幸蜀回乃知微防深叹异之 罗公逺多秘术最善形之法上就公逺虽传受不肯尽其要上毎与同为之则没人不能知若自试或余衣带防露幞头脚每被宫人知上所在上怒命力士裹以油幞置榨木下压杀而埋弃之不旬日有中使自蜀道回逢公逺于路乗骡而笑谓使者曰上之为戱一何虐耶

万回师阌郷人也神用不足谓愚而痴无所知虽父母亦以豚犬畜之兄被戍役安西音问隔絶父母谓其诚死日夕涕泣而忧思也万回顾父感念甚忽跪而言曰涕泣岂非忧兄也父母曰信然万回曰详思我兄所要者衣装糗粮巾之属悉备之某将觐焉忽一日朝赍所备夕返其家告父母曰兄平善矣发书视之乃兄迹也一家异之农抵安西万余 里以其万里而回故谓之万回也居常貌如愚痴忽有先觉异见惊人神异也上在藩邸或游行人间万回于聚落街衢髙声曰天子来或曰圣人来其处信宿间上必经过徘徊也安乐公主上之季妹也附防韦氏可炙手道路惧焉万回望其车骑道唾曰血腥不可近也不旋踵而灭亡之祸及矣上知万回非常人内出二宫人日夕侍奉特勑于集贤院图形焉

道士叶法善精于符箓之术上累拜为鸿胪卿优礼待焉法善居真观尝有朝客数十人诣之解带淹留满座思酒忽有人叩门云麴秀才法善令人谓曰方有朝僚未暇瞻晤幸吾子异日见临也语未毕有一美措傲睨而入年二十余肥白可观笑揖诸公居末席抗声谈论援引古人一席不测恐耸观之良乆蹔起旋转法善谓诸公曰此子突入语辩如此岂非魃魅为惑乎试与诸公避之麴生复至扼腕抵掌论难锋起势不可当法善宻以小劒击之随手失坠于阶下化为瓶榼一座惊慑遽视其所乃盈瓶醲醖也咸大笑饮之其味甚嘉座客醉而揖其瓶曰麴生风味不可忘也 上命裴寛为河南尹寛性好释氏师事普寂禅师旦夕造谒焉居一日寛诣寂寂曰有少事未暇欵语且请迟回休憇也寛乃屏賔从止于空室见寂洁涤正堂焚香端坐坐未乆忽闻扣门连声云一行天师至一行入诣作礼礼寂之足礼讫附耳宻语其貌絶恭寂但顾云无不可者语讫又礼礼语如是三寂惟云是是一行语讫降堦入南堂自阖其扉寂乃徐命弟子云遣声钟一行和尚

灭度矣左右疾走视之一如其言后寂灭度寛复衰绖葬之日徒步出城送之甚为缙绅所讥也寛子谞复为河南尹素好谈谐多异笔尝有投牒误书背谞判云者畔似那畔那畔似者畔我不可辞与你判笑杀门前着靴汉又有妇人投状争猫儿状云是猫儿即是儿猫不是儿猫即不是猫儿谞大笑判状云猫儿不识主傍我搦老防两家不须争将来与裴谞遂纳其猫儿争者亦哂 安禄山初为张韩公帐下走使之吏韩常令禄山洗足韩公脚下有黒防子禄山因洗脚而窃窥之韩公顾笑曰黒子吾贵相也独汝窥之亦能有之乎禄山曰某贱人也不幸两足皆有比将军者黒而加大竟不知是何祥也韩公竒而观之益亲厚之约为义儿而加荐宠焉无畏三蔵自天竺至所由引谒上见而敬信焉上谓三蔵曰师自逺而来困倦欲于何方休息耶三蔵进曰臣在天竺国时闻西明寺宣律师持律第一愿依止焉上可之宣律禁诫坚苦焚修精洁三蔵饮酒食肉言行麤易

徃往乗醉而喧秽汚絪席宣律颇不甘心忽中夜宣律扪虱将投于地三蔵半醉连声呼曰律师扑杀佛子宣律方知是神异人也整衣作礼投而师事之宣律精苦之甚常夜行道临堦坠堕忽觉有人捧承其足宣律顾视之乃少年也宣律遽问弟子何人中夜在此少年曰某非常人即毗沙王之子那咤太子也防法之故拥防和尚乆矣宣律曰贫道修行无事烦太子威神自在西域有可作佛事者愿太子致之太子曰某有佛牙寳事虽乆头目犹舍敢不奉献宣律求之即今崇圣寺佛牙是也 太真妃最善于击磬搏拊之音泠泠然新声虽太常棃园之能人莫加也上令采蓝田緑玉琢为器上进簨簴流苏之属皆以金钿珠翠珍怪之物杂饰之又铸二金狮子作拏攫腾奋之状各重二百余斤以扶其他防绘缛丽制作神妙一时无比也上幸蜀回京师乐器多亡失独玊磬偶在上顾之凄然不忍置于前促令送太常至今蔵于太常正乐库

上所幸美人忽梦人邀去纵酒宻防任饮尽而归归辄流汗倦怠忽忽后因从容尽白于上上曰此必术人所为也汝若复往但随宜以物识之其夕熟寐飘然又徃半醉见石砚在前乃宻印手文于曲房屏风上寤而具啓上上乃潜以物色令于诸宫观求之异日于东门观得其屏风手文尚在道士已遁矣 安西衙将刘文口辩善奏对上每嘉之文树髭生颔下貌猿猴上令黄幡绰嘲之文树切恶猿猴之号乃宻赂幡绰祈不言之幡绰讯而进嘲曰可怜好文树髭须共颏頥别任文树靣孔不似猢孙猢孙强似文树上知其赂遗大笑之 平康坊南街废蛮院即李林甫旧宅也林甫于正堂后别创一堂制度弯曲有却月之形名曰月堂木土秀丽精巧当时莫俦也林甫每欲破灭人家即入月堂精思极虑喜恱而出必不存焉及将败林甫于堂上见一物如人动遍体被毛毛如猪立锯牙钩爪三尺余以击林甫目如电光而怒视之林甫连叱不动遂命弧矢毛人笑而跳入前堂堂中青衣遇而暴卒经于廐中善马皆死不累日而林甫卒

太真妃常因妬媚有语侵上上怒甚召髙力士以辎軿送其家妃悔恨号泣抽刀剪发授力士曰珠玉珍异皆上所赐不足充献惟发父母所生可达妾意望持此伸妾万一慕恋之诚上得髪挥涕悯然遽命力士召归天寳初上游华清宫有刘朝霞者献贺幸温泉赋词调倜傥杂以俳谐文多不载今畧其词曰若夫天寳二年十月后兮腊月前办有司之供具命驾幸于温泉天门干开露神僊之辐凑銮舆划出驱甲仗以骈阗 青一队兮黄一队熊踏胷兮豹拏背朱一团兮绣一团玊镂珂兮金镂鞍述徳云直攫得盘古髓搯得女娲瓤遮莫尔古时千帝岂如我今日三郎其自叙云别有穷竒蹭蹬失路猖狂骨懂虽短伎艺能长梦里防回富贵觉来依旧凄惶今日是千年一遇叩头莫五角六张帝覧而竒之将加贺上命朝霞改去五角六张字奏云臣草此赋诗有神助自谓文不加防笔不停缀不愿从天而改上顾曰真穷薄人也授以春官卫上左焉 开天传信记
